1: The address shifts to the plural 'sons' (bānim), suggesting a wisdom teacher addressing students, though the singular 'father's instruction' (mūsar 'āb) maintains the family model. Mūsar is Proverbs' governing term for educational discipline — encompassing instruction, correction, and the character formed by it; Greek paideia captures it best.
2: Leqaḥ (teaching/what is received) is what the student takes away; tōrāh here means instruction or guidance rather than the Mosaic law specifically. Both terms denote received wisdom rather than innate insight.
3: Yāḥîd (only/tender) is rare in this sense; the parallel 'beloved' (rāk, tender) creates a picture of childhood cherishing. The father recalls being his father's treasured pupil — wisdom is transmitted person to person, not merely text to reader.
4: Yiḥzaq — 'lay hold of' — is the same root used of Boaz 'catching' Ruth's hand; the grip on wisdom must be as firm as a physical hold. The negative command 'do not let your heart let go' (al-yirepaḥ libbẹkā) frames wisdom as something that can slip away.
5: Qenēh (get/acquire) appears in both v5 and v7 — wisdom is framed as a commodity to be obtained with effort. The same verb in v7 creates wordplay: 'the beginning of wisdom: get wisdom.' This does not mean wisdom is for sale but that it requires active pursuit.
6: Personified wisdom (ḥokmāh) becomes a guardian: 'she will keep you' (tiŝmerẹkā). The protective-companion imagery begins here and culminates in Proverbs 8's full personification. The condition is simply 'do not forsake her' — wisdom is lost by neglect, not by attack.
7: 'The beginning of wisdom: get wisdom' (rē'šît ḥokmāh qenēh ḥokmāh) is intentionally circular and emphatic: the first, most important thing you can do is pursue wisdom itself. Rē'šît can mean 'beginning', 'first in importance', or 'principal thing' — likely all three here.
8: Salselehā (exalt her) is a hapax in the Piel, probably meaning 'lift up' or 'honor' — what you prize, you are prized by. The reciprocity of the wisdom relationship (honor wisdom → be honored by wisdom) runs through Proverbs 1–9's extended wisdom-personification.
9: Wisdom gives a 'garland of grace' (livyat ḥēn) and a 'crown of glory' (keteret tipʾāret) — both royal-martial imagery applied to the wise person. The MKT preserves 'garland' and 'crown' for these concrete terms rather than abstracting to 'honor.'
10: The father promises that receiving (qabbēl) his words will multiply the son's years. Long life as the reward of wisdom is a recurring Proverbs motif; Deuteronomy uses the same promise for Torah observance, grounding the wisdom tradition in covenant theology.
11: 'I have taught you' (hôrêtîkā) and 'I have led you' (hidractîkā) use different verbs for instruction and guidance — the combination covers both theoretical teaching and practical mentoring, both knowing the way and walking it.
12: Tsārar (be hampered/narrow) — 'your step will not be cramped/hampered.' The image is of unimpeded walking: wisdom gives sure footing and spacious movement. The antithesis — stumbling (tikasel) — describes the gait of the wicked in v19.
13: 'Hold fast to discipline' (hāzēq bammûsār) — the same grip vocabulary as v4. The climactic identification: hî' ḥayyêkā = 'she is your life.' Wisdom is not merely instrumental to good living; she is life itself. The NT identification of Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24) takes up this life-identification directly.
14: The father turns to negative commands: 'do not enter the path of the wicked' (al-tābô' bĕ'oraḥ rĕšā'îm). The two-way motif — wisdom's path vs. the path of the wicked — structures all of Proverbs 4.
15: Perā'ēhû — 'shun it/avoid it' — is intensive: not just 'don't go there' but 'actively turn away from it.' The fourfold imperative in v15 (shun, do not travel it, turn away, pass on) underscores the urgency of avoidance.
16: The portrait of the wicked is relentlessly active: they cannot sleep unless they do evil — wickedness is compulsive, not merely chosen. Nigrĕlāh šēnātam = 'their sleep is robbed/taken away' if they have not caused someone to stumble. This describes addiction to wrongdoing.
17: 'They eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence' (leḥem riš'āh... yêyn ḥamāsîm) — the dietary metaphor describes evil as sustenance: the wicked are nourished by what destroys others. Ḥāmās (violence) is the word used for the wickedness that prompted the flood (Genesis 6:11).
18: Nōgah (brightness/gleaming) and the participle hālōk wā'ōr (going and shining/brightening) describe the progressive illumination of the righteous path. The comparison to 'the full day' (kēdōn hayyôm) — perhaps 'midday' — captures the trajectory: not a fixed light but an increasing one.
19: The wicked path is 'ăpēlâ (deep darkness/gloom) — the antithesis of the brightening light of v18. Bammeh yikkāšēlû lō' yāde'û — 'on what they stumble they do not know' — captures the moral blindness of those who have chosen darkness: they cannot even identify their own stumbling blocks.
20: The father returns to the positive imperatives: haqšēb (incline/attend) and hiṭṭēh (turn/bow). Hearing wisdom requires physical posture — ears inclined, heart directed. The teaching presupposes not passive reception but active, directed attention.
21: 'Let them not escape from your eyes' (al-yāliṣûm mê'êneyḵā) — keep wisdom in sight. The counterpart to the eyes not straying (v25) is wisdom staying within your field of vision. Šomerem bĕtôk libbẹḵā = 'guard them in the center of your heart.'
22: Ḥayyîm hēm lĕmōṣê'êhem — 'life they are for those who find them' — repeating the life-identification of v13 but now applied to the specific 'words' and 'sayings' of v20. Wisdom's words have life-giving power; marpe' (healing/health) points to their curative as well as preserving function.
23: 'Guard your heart above all else' (mikkol-mišmār nĕṣōr libbẹkā) — the most concentrated command in the father's discourse. Tôṣe'ôt ḥayyîm = 'the outflowings/issues of life' — the heart is the source from which all of life flows. MKT 'source from which life flows' captures both the outflow image and the causal primacy of the heart over all behavior.
24: 'Remove from you crookedness of mouth' (hāsēr mimmẹkā 'iqqeš peh); 'iqqēš (crooked/perverse) describes the twisted mouth — speech that distorts reality. The imperative to remove crooked speech flows directly from the previous verse: what flows from a guarded heart is straight speech.
25: 'Let your eyes look directly ahead' (êneyḵā lĕnōkaḥ yabiṭû) — the father links right vision with right walking. Nōkaḥ = directly/straight before you; le'umat = right in front. The disciplined gaze that does not wander is the complement to the guarded heart.
26: Peles (balance/level) is the root of the call to 'ponder/level' the path of your feet. The MKT 'ponder' captures both the reflective and the smoothing/leveling senses. The disciplined life is not impulsive but measured — paths are consciously assessed before being taken.
27: 'Do not swerve right or left' (al-tiṭ yāmîn ûśmō'l) — the final verse of Proverbs 4 frames the entire discourse with the two-way imagery: the righteous path is straight, and departures in either direction lead away from it. Hāsēr raglĕḵā mērā' = 'turn your foot away from evil.'
